title: CSP allows script-src 'unsafe-inline' (and style-src 'unsafe-inline'), defeating XSS containment
url: N/A (applies to every response via headers())
description: The Content-Security-Policy sets script-src and style-src to include 'unsafe-inline'. 'unsafe-inline' on script-src means any injected inline <script> or event-handler attribute executes, so the CSP provides no meaningful defence-in-depth against XSS. For a PII app (iqama, IBAN, salary) an XSS payload could read the DOM/tRPC responses and exfiltrate. 'unsafe-eval' was correctly dropped and the code comment acknowledges a pending nonce rollout, but until nonces ship the primary XSS mitigation is absent.
expectedBehavior: script-src should use per-request nonces or hashes (no 'unsafe-inline'); style-src should move to nonces/hashes where feasible.

evidence.snippet: "script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'",
"style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'",

reproduction: Load any page, inspect the Content-Security-Policy response header; observe script-src includes 'unsafe-inline'.
technicalRisk: A single stored/reflected XSS becomes fully weaponizable (session/PII exfiltration) with no CSP brake.
businessRisk: PDPL breach exposure if employee PII is exfiltrated via injected script; reputational damage.
recommendation: Adopt Next.js nonce-based CSP (middleware sets a per-request nonce, propagate to inline scripts) and remove 'unsafe-inline' from script-src. Migrate inline styles to nonces/hashes or external stylesheets.
workaround: Keep object-src 'none', frame-ancestors 'none', base-uri 'self' (already present) and prioritise the nonce rollout the comment references.

title: Permissive CSP connect-src 'https:' and img-src 'https:' allow PII exfiltration to any HTTPS host
description: connect-src permits 'https:' (any TLS origin) and img-src permits 'https:' (any TLS origin). Combined with script-src 'unsafe-inline' (PRIV-001), an injected script can beacon PII to an attacker-controlled https endpoint (fetch or an pixel with data in the query string) without CSP interference.
expectedBehavior: connect-src and img-src should enumerate the specific origins the app actually needs (self + Supabase + Perplexity CDN + Sentry).
actualBehavior: connect-src 'self' https: and img-src 'self' blob: data: https: are wide open to all HTTPS hosts.

evidence.snippet: "img-src 'self' blob: data: https:",
"connect-src 'self' https:",

reproduction: Inspect CSP header; note connect-src/img-src accept arbitrary https origins.
technicalRisk: Removes CSP's exfiltration-channel restriction; any injected code can phone home.
businessRisk: Amplifies impact of any XSS into a data-exfiltration event.
recommendation: Replace 'https:' with an explicit allow-list of required origins for connect-src and img-src.

title: Real production Supabase DB connection string (with password) stored in cleartext file in repo working tree
description: _db_url.txt at the repo root contains a live Supabase pooler connection string including the database password and the tenant search_path (rukn_energy_services). The file is listed in .gitignore (so it is not committed to git history that was checked), but it sits in the developer working tree in plaintext, is trivially shared via backups/zips/screen-shares, and grants direct SQL access to production PII (iqama, IBAN, salary). A credential of this sensitivity must not live in a loose text file.
expectedBehavior: Production DB credentials live only in a secret manager / Vercel env vars, never in a working-tree file.
actualBehavior: Full production connection string with password is readable from _db_url.txt.

evidence.snippet: postgresql://postgres.iefwhxxhrycaalhxkfgp:0uKN********@aws-1-ap-south-1.pooler.supabase.com:5432/postgres?options=-csearch_path%3Drukn_energy_services

reproduction: Open _db_url.txt at repo root; the password is present in cleartext.
technicalRisk: Anyone obtaining the working tree gains full read/write access to the production tenant database.
businessRisk: Direct PDPL breach vector; unauthorized access to all tenant PII and payroll data.
recommendation: Rotate the Supabase database password immediately, delete _db_url.txt from disk, and confirm it never entered git history (git log --all -- _db_url.txt). Store the URL only in the secret manager.
workaround: Delete the file now; rotate the credential regardless.

title: Vercel OIDC JWT tokens stored in local .env.local / .env.production.local
description: VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN (a signed JWT) is present in both .env.local and .env.production.local. These files are gitignored and the tokens are short-lived (exp claim ~12h from iat), so residual risk is low, but they are auto-generated bearer tokens sitting on disk.
expectedBehavior: Ephemeral OIDC tokens are fine when gitignored and short-lived; ensure they are never committed or shared.
actualBehavior: Tokens present on disk (gitignored, not committed).

evidence.snippet: VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN="eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVC....(short-lived JWT)"

technicalRisk: If a token is exfiltrated before expiry it could be replayed against Vercel OIDC-scoped resources.
recommendation: No action beyond confirming .gitignore coverage (already present). Do not paste these files into shared channels.

title: Password-reset token and user email written to server logs (account-takeover via log access)
description: The request-reset handler logs the full reset URL (which embeds the raw single-use reset token) together with the user's email via console.info. Server logs are typically retained and broadly readable (Vercel log drains, observability tools). Anyone with log access can read the token within its 1-hour TTL and complete an account takeover; the email is also PII exposed in logs. The DB correctly stores only the token hash, so the log is the weak link.
expectedBehavior: Reset links must be delivered only to the user's mailbox and never logged; at most log a non-sensitive event id.
actualBehavior: console.info("[auth] password reset link generated for", email, resetUrl) logs email + raw token.

evidence.snippet: const resetUrl = `${base}/reset-password?email=${encodeURIComponent(email)}&token=${rawToken}`;
console.info("[auth] password reset link generated for", email, resetUrl);

reproduction: Call POST /api/auth/request-reset with a registered email; inspect server logs for the reset URL containing token and email.
technicalRisk: Log reader obtains a valid reset token and hijacks the account.
businessRisk: Account takeover of HR/admin accounts; PDPL breach of PII in logs.
recommendation: Wire the email delivery (TODO(email)) and remove the console.info entirely, or gate it behind NODE_ENV !== 'production' with the token redacted. Never log tokens or email.
workaround: Immediately restrict log access and set short log retention until fixed.

title: User email placed in reset URL query string (leaks via history / referrer)
description: The reset link carries the user's email as a query parameter. Query strings land in browser history, proxy logs, and (for any off-site navigation) the Referer header. Email is PII under PDPL and need not be in the URL — the token alone identifies the request server-side.
expectedBehavior: Identify the reset by token only; resolve the email server-side from the token record.
actualBehavior: email is included as a query parameter alongside the token.

evidence.snippet: const resetUrl = `${base}/reset-password?email=${encodeURIComponent(email)}&token=${rawToken}`;

reproduction: Generate a reset link; observe email present in the URL.
recommendation: Drop the email param; look it up from the verificationTokens row by token hash during reset.

title: Authentication failures log user email (PII) via console, bypassing the redacting logger
description: Failed-login and MFA-failure paths log the account email through console.warn. A redacting pino logger exists in packages/config (redacts authorization/cookie/password) but is not used here, and its redact list does not cover email or PII fields anyway. Emails are PII and accumulate in logs on every failed attempt.
expectedBehavior: Log a hashed/opaque account identifier, not the raw email; route through the redacting logger.
actualBehavior: console.warn('[auth] failed login attempt for', email) and MFA-failure log the email.

evidence.snippet: console.warn('[auth] failed login attempt for', email);
console.warn('[auth] MFA verification failed for', email);
redact: ["req.headers.authorization", "req.headers.cookie", "body.password"],

reproduction: Trigger a failed login; observe email in server logs.
technicalRisk: PII (and enumeration signal) accumulates in logs.
recommendation: Remove email from these log lines (or hash it) and route auth logging through the redacting logger; extend redact paths to cover PII.

title: No data-subject rectification / erasure / consent-withdrawal endpoints (PDPL rights incomplete)
description: A PDPL data-subject access (export) right is implemented (employee.exportMyData, audited). However there is no self-service or documented workflow for the right to correction, the right to destruction/erasure, or withdrawal of consent. Employee deletion is soft-delete only (status=terminated) which by design retains all PII indefinitely, and no anonymisation/retention-expiry job exists. Saudi PDPL grants data subjects rights to access, correction, and destruction; only access is covered in code.
expectedBehavior: Provide (or document a controlled process for) correction, erasure/anonymisation, consent withdrawal, and a defined retention/disposal schedule.
actualBehavior: Only exportMyData exists; soft-delete retains PII with no erasure/anonymisation path.

evidence.snippet: exportMyData: protectedProcedure.query(async ({ ctx }) => {  // PDPL access request
// Soft-delete only ... Hard deletion is intentionally not exposed.

reproduction: Search routers for erasure/rectification/consent-withdrawal procedures; none exist.
technicalRisk: Cannot fulfil a data-subject destruction/correction request within code.
recommendation: Add correction and erasure/anonymisation workflows (audited), a consent-withdrawal path, and a documented retention & disposal policy. Legal review required to define lawful retention periods (payroll/tax records vs erasable PII).
workaround: Document a manual, audited DSR handling process in the interim.

title: Consent capture labelled 'GDPR' in a Saudi PDPL product
description: Candidate creation captures explicit consent (a genuine strength), but the field and validation message are named for GDPR while the product's governing regime is Saudi PDPL. The consent language/basis should reference PDPL to be accurate for the jurisdiction.
expectedBehavior: Consent wording and field naming reference the applicable PDPL basis.
actualBehavior: Field named gdprConsent with a GDPR-worded validation message.

evidence.snippet: e.gdprConsent = "GDPR consent is required to store candidate data";
I confirm that this candidate has provided explicit consent to have their

reproduction: Open candidate create form; consent label references GDPR.
businessRisk: Consent record may not map to PDPL lawful-basis requirements; audit optics.
recommendation: Rename to reflect PDPL and have legal confirm the consent wording/basis for KSA.

title: Salary certificate / experience letter generation exposes decrypted iqama + full salary and is not audited
description: document.generateLetter builds a salary certificate / experience letter containing the employee's decrypted national ID (iqama) and full basic/housing/transport salary. This is a sensitive PII/financial export, but no writeAudit call is made, so there is no record of who generated a document containing another person's iqama and salary or when. writeAudit is used for employee/payroll/settlement mutations but not for this read/export.
expectedBehavior: Every generation/export of a document containing another person's PII/salary is audited (actor, subject, type, timestamp).
actualBehavior: generateLetter returns HTML with iqama + salary and writes no audit entry.

evidence.snippet: nationalId: employee.iqamaNumberEnc,
basicSalary: Number(employee.salaryBasic),
return { html, type: input.type };  // no writeAudit(...)

reproduction: Call document.generateLetter; check audit_logs — no entry recorded for the export.
technicalRisk: PII/salary disclosure with no attributable trail.
businessRisk: PDPL accountability gap; cannot investigate misuse of salary certificates.
recommendation: Add writeAudit('document.generate', entityType 'employee', subject id, {type}) to generateLetter; consider the same for any PII-bearing read/export.

title: Audit log omits authentication, role-assignment, export, and PII-view events
description: writeAudit is invoked only for employee create/update/delete, payroll runs, settlement, and PDPL self-export. Security- and privacy-relevant events are NOT persisted to audit_logs: successful/failed logins and lockouts (only ephemeral console.warn), role assignment/changes via invites, MFA enable/disable, document/letter generation (PRIV-010), and PII/salary views. PDPL accountability and standard security monitoring expect durable auth and access-to-PII trails.
expectedBehavior: Login/logout, lockout, role changes, MFA changes, config changes, and PII/export access are written to a durable audit trail with actor/action/target/timestamp.
actualBehavior: Auth events are only console-logged; role/MFA/export/view events are not audited.

evidence.snippet: export const auditRouter = createTRPCRouter({ list: requireRole("super_admin", "hr_manager") ... })
console.warn('[auth] failed login attempt for', email);  // not persisted to audit_logs
role: role ?? "employee",  // role assignment, no writeAudit

reproduction: Log in, assign a role via invite, generate a letter; query audit_logs — none of these appear.
technicalRisk: No forensic trail for authentication abuse, privilege changes, or PII access.
businessRisk: PDPL accountability gap; incident investigation and breach reporting hampered.
recommendation: Emit audit_logs entries for auth (login/logout/lockout/MFA), role/permission changes, config changes, and PII exports/views. Keep the existing tenant-scoped, read-only audit read surface.
workaround: Ship auth events to a durable log store as an interim measure.

title: Notifications: no unsubscribe / channel preferences; payslip email exposes net pay
description: The notifications table supports email/sms/in_app channels but there is no user preference/unsubscribe surface — a data subject cannot opt out of a channel. The PayslipReady email template renders net pay ('{netPay} SAR') in the message body, so salary lands in the mail transport/inbox if/when dispatch is wired. Note: no email dispatch (resend.emails.send) is currently implemented anywhere, so templates are defined but unsent; this is both a functional gap and a reason to fix the payslip content before go-live.
expectedBehavior: Per-user notification preferences/opt-out; sensitive emails avoid embedding salary figures (link to the app instead).
actualBehavior: No preferences model; payslip email body includes net pay; no dispatch implemented.

evidence.snippet: <Text style={styles.netPayValue}>{netPay} SAR</Text>
export const notificationChannelEnum = ["email", "sms", "in_app"] as const;  // no user-preference table

reproduction: Review notification router/schema for a preferences/unsubscribe surface (none); review payslip template for net pay.
technicalRisk: Salary disclosure in email transport; no opt-out.
businessRisk: PDPL data-minimisation and consent-management gaps once email is enabled.
recommendation: Add a per-user notification-preferences table with opt-out and an unsubscribe path; remove the salary figure from the payslip email (link to the secured payslip instead) before wiring dispatch.
workaround: Keep email dispatch disabled until content/preferences are addressed.

title: Audit log retains salary old/new values as plaintext JSON
description: For salary changes, writeAudit records old/new salary values in the audit_logs.newValue / oldValue jsonb columns in plaintext. This is inherent to an audit trail and access is restricted (HR Manager / super admin, tenant-scoped), but note the employee salary/PII fields are encrypted at rest in the employees table while their historical values sit unencrypted in audit_logs. Any DB-level exposure of audit_logs discloses salary history.
expectedBehavior: Sensitive audit payloads are protected commensurately with the source columns (access control at minimum; consider encryption/tokenisation for salary snapshots).
actualBehavior: Salary before/after values stored as plaintext jsonb in audit_logs.

evidence.snippet: oldValue: pickChanged(current as Record<string, unknown>, data), newValue: data,  // includes salary fields
oldValue: jsonb("old_value"), newValue: jsonb("new_value"),

reproduction: Change a salary; inspect audit_logs row — salary values are plaintext.
technicalRisk: DB compromise of audit_logs discloses salary history that is encrypted elsewhere.
businessRisk: Residual PII exposure surface; inconsistent protection of the same data.
recommendation: Restrict audit_logs at the DB layer; consider encrypting or masking salary snapshots in audit payloads, or storing field-change markers instead of raw values where full fidelity is not required.
workaround: Ensure audit_logs access is tightly controlled and monitored.
